items.mistake: Always defaulting to RBF without trying a linear kernel
items.fix: Start with a linear kernel on high-dimensional or sparse features; move to RBF/poly only when validation shows underfitting.
items.why: Linear K is cheaper and often optimal when the feature space is already rich (e.g. TF-IDF).
items.example: On 20k-dimensional sparse text, linear SVC may match RBF accuracy in 1/10th the train time.

items.fix: Search γ on a log grid (e.g. 1e-4 … 1e1) with cross-validation; prefer 'scale' or 'auto' as baselines.
items.why: Large γ makes K spike only at training points — the model memorizes noise.
items.example: γ=100 on 150 samples → 100% train / 60% val; γ=0.1 → 85% train / 78% val.

items.mistake: Using kernels on unscaled numeric features
items.fix: Standardize or scale continuous columns before RBF or polynomial kernels so no single feature dominates ‖x−x′‖ or x·x′.
items.why: RBF and dot-product kernels are not scale-invariant; one large-magnitude column distorts geometry.
items.example: Income in dollars vs age in years: StandardScaler on both before SVC(kernel='rbf').

items.mistake: Assuming any similarity formula is a valid kernel
items.fix: Use library kernels or prove positive semi-definiteness; check eigenvalues of a small Gram matrix if experimenting.
items.why: Invalid K can yield indefinite matrices and unstable or meaningless SVM solutions.
items.example: K(x,x′)=−‖x−x′‖ fails PSD; use RBF exp(−γ‖x−x′‖²) instead.
